CONFIDENTIAL --- PROVIDED IN CONNECTION WITH DUE DILIGENCE FOR PROPOSED ACQUISITION OF PRISM HEALTH ANALYTICS, INC.

**AMENDED AND RESTATED OPERATING AGREEMENT**

**OF**

**BEACON INSIGHTS, LLC**

**A North Carolina Limited Liability Company**

*Selected Provisions --- Excerpted for Transaction Review Purposes*

Effective Date: August 1, 2018

Originally formed by filing of Articles of Organization with the North Carolina Secretary of State on March 22, 2018.

Employer Identification Number: 84-3219876

This document contains selected provisions only. Sections not relevant to the transaction review have been omitted and are marked with \"\[Provisions Omitted\]\" where they would otherwise appear.

**[Preamble]{.underline}**

This Amended and Restated Operating Agreement (this \"Agreement\") of Beacon Insights, LLC, a North Carolina limited liability company (the \"Company\"), is entered into effective as of August 1, 2018 (the \"Effective Date\"), by and among the Members listed on Schedule A attached hereto.

**RECITALS**

**WHEREAS**, the Company was organized under the North Carolina Limited Liability Company Act (N.C. Gen. Stat. § 57D-1-01 et seq.) by the filing of Articles of Organization with the North Carolina Secretary of State on March 22, 2018;

**WHEREAS**, the initial members of the Company entered into an Operating Agreement dated as of March 22, 2018 (the \"Original Operating Agreement\"), and the Members now desire to amend and restate the Original Operating Agreement in its entirety as set forth herein;

**WHEREAS**, the Company is engaged in the business of providing data analytics, data engineering, and healthcare data insights services to healthcare providers, payors, and related entities (the \"Business\"); and

**WHEREAS**, the Company intends to be classified as a partnership for federal and state income tax purposes and shall not elect to be treated as a corporation under Treasury Regulation Section 301.7701-3;

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Members agree as follows:

**[Article I --- Definitions]{.underline}**

As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

**\"Affiliate\"** means, with respect to any Person, any other Person directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by, or under common control with such Person. For purposes of this definition, \"control\" means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. For the avoidance of doubt, Prism Health Analytics, Inc. and its subsidiaries are Affiliates of the Company.

**\"Capital Account\"** means the capital account maintained for each Member in accordance with Section 704(b) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended (the \"Code\"), and Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-1(b)(2)(iv).

**\"Change of Control\"** means, with respect to any Member, (a) a merger, consolidation, or other business combination transaction involving such Member as a result of which the equity holders of such Member immediately prior to such transaction hold less than fifty percent (50%) of the voting equity interests of the surviving entity, (b) a sale, transfer, or other disposition of all or substantially all of the assets of such Member, or (c) any other transaction or series of related transactions resulting in a change in the beneficial ownership of more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting equity interests of such Member.

**\"Company\"** means Beacon Insights, LLC, a North Carolina limited liability company.

**\"Fair Market Value\"** means the price at which a willing buyer and a willing seller, neither being under any compulsion to buy or sell, and both having reasonable knowledge of relevant facts, would agree to transact, as determined in accordance with Section 8.04 of this Agreement.

**\"Fiscal Year\"** means the calendar year ending December 31.

**\"Intellectual Property\"** or **\"IP\"** means all patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, software, databases, proprietary data sets, algorithms, and other intangible property.

**\"IP License Agreement\"** means that certain Intellectual Property License Agreement dated August 1, 2018, between Prism Health Analytics, Inc. and the Company, as amended from time to time.

**\"Majority Interest\"** means Members holding more than fifty percent (50%) of the aggregate Percentage Interests.

**\"Management Services Agreement\"** means that certain Management Services Agreement dated August 1, 2018, between Prism Health Analytics, Inc. and the Company, as amended from time to time.

**\"Member\"** means each Person listed on Schedule A hereto, as updated from time to time, who holds a Membership Interest in the Company.

**\"Membership Interest\"** means the entire ownership interest of a Member in the Company, including such Member\'s rights to allocations of profits and losses, rights to distributions, and rights to vote or participate in management as set forth in this Agreement.

**\"Percentage Interest\"** means, with respect to each Member, the percentage set forth opposite such Member\'s name on Schedule A hereto.

**\"Person\"** means any individual, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, trust, estate, or other entity.

**\"ROFR Notice\"** has the meaning set forth in Section 8.03(b).

**\"Transfer\"** means any direct or indirect sale, assignment, pledge, encumbrance, hypothecation, gift, or other disposition of any kind, whether voluntary or involuntary, by operation of law or otherwise.

**\"Unrealized Receivables\"** shall have the meaning set forth in Section 751(c) of the Code.

**[Article II --- Formation and Purpose]{.underline}**

**Section 2.01 --- Formation.** The Company was formed as a North Carolina limited liability company on March 22, 2018, by the filing of Articles of Organization with the North Carolina Secretary of State in accordance with the North Carolina Limited Liability Company Act, N.C. Gen. Stat. § 57D-1-01 et seq. (the \"Act\").

**Section 2.02 --- Name.** The name of the Company is Beacon Insights, LLC.

**Section 2.03 --- Principal Office.** The principal office of the Company shall be at 4200 Lake Boone Trail, Suite 520, Raleigh, North Carolina 27607, or such other place as the Manager may from time to time designate.

**Section 2.04 --- Purpose.** The purpose of the Company is to engage in the business of providing data analytics, data engineering, healthcare data insights services, and any lawful act or activity for which limited liability companies may be formed under the Act.

**Section 2.05 --- Tax Classification.** The Company shall be classified as a partnership for federal and state income tax purposes. The Company shall not file an election under Treasury Regulation Section 301.7701-3 to be classified as a corporation or as an entity disregarded as separate from its owner.

**Section 2.06 --- Term.** The Company shall have perpetual existence unless dissolved in accordance with Article X of this Agreement.

**[Article III --- Members; Capital Contributions; Membership Interests]{.underline}**

**Section 3.01 --- Members.** The Members and their respective Percentage Interests as of the Effective Date are set forth on Schedule A attached hereto.

**Section 3.02 --- Capital Contributions.** The initial capital contributions of the Members are set forth on Schedule A. No Member shall be required to make additional capital contributions to the Company except upon the affirmative vote of all Members.

**Section 3.03 --- Percentage Interests.** The Percentage Interests of the Members are as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Member**                          **Percentage Interest**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Prism Health Analytics, Inc.        80.5%

  Marcus Delano                       12.0%

  Shirin Avesta                       7.5%

  **Total**                           **100.0%**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

**Section 3.04 --- No Withdrawal.** No Member shall have the right to withdraw capital from the Company except as provided in Article VII of this Agreement.

**Section 3.05 --- Capital Accounts.** A separate Capital Account shall be maintained for each Member in accordance with Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-1(b)(2)(iv). Each Member\'s Capital Account shall be (a) increased by the amount of such Member\'s capital contributions, such Member\'s distributive share of Net Profits, and any items of income or gain specially allocated to such Member pursuant to Section 6.02, and (b) decreased by the amount of cash and the Fair Market Value of any property distributed to such Member, such Member\'s distributive share of Net Losses, and any items of deduction or loss specially allocated to such Member pursuant to Section 6.02.

**[Article IV --- Management]{.underline}**

**Section 4.01 --- Manager-Managed.** The Company shall be manager-managed. Prism Health Analytics, Inc. shall serve as the sole Manager of the Company (the \"Manager\"). The Manager shall serve in such capacity until its resignation, removal, or dissolution.

**Section 4.02 --- Authority of Manager.** The Manager shall have full and exclusive authority and power to manage and control the business and affairs of the Company, including, without limitation, the authority to execute contracts, hire and terminate employees and independent contractors, make ordinary course expenditures, manage day-to-day operations, and take all actions necessary or advisable in furtherance of the Business.

**Section 4.03 --- Actions Requiring Member Approval.** Notwithstanding Section 4.02, the following actions shall require the prior approval of Members holding at least a Majority Interest:

> \(a\) any merger, consolidation, or dissolution of the Company;
>
> \(b\) any sale of all or substantially all of the Company\'s assets;
>
> \(c\) any amendment to this Agreement;
>
> \(d\) any issuance of additional Membership Interests or admission of new Members;
>
> \(e\) entry into any transaction with the Manager or any Affiliate of the Manager other than as contemplated by the Management Services Agreement and the IP License Agreement.

**Section 4.04 --- Officers.** The Manager may from time to time appoint one or more officers of the Company, including a President, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer, and may delegate to such officers such authority and duties as the Manager may determine.

\[Provisions Omitted\]

**[Article V --- \[Provisions Omitted\]]{.underline}**

\[Provisions regarding indemnification, insurance, and liability omitted.\]

**[Article VI --- Allocations of Profits and Losses]{.underline}**

**Section 6.01 --- Net Profits and Net Losses.** Net Profits and Net Losses of the Company for each Fiscal Year shall be allocated among the Members in proportion to their respective Percentage Interests, except as otherwise required by the following provisions of this Article VI.

**Section 6.02 --- Special Allocations.**

> \(a\) *Qualified Income Offset.* In the event any Member unexpectedly receives any adjustments, allocations, or distributions described in Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-1(b)(2)(ii)(d)(4), (5), or (6), items of Company income and gain shall be specially allocated to each such Member in an amount and manner sufficient to eliminate, to the extent required by such Treasury Regulation, the adjusted capital account deficit of such Member as quickly as possible.
>
> \(b\) *Gross Income Allocation.* If any Member has an adjusted capital account deficit at the end of any Fiscal Year, such Member shall be specially allocated items of Company gross income and gain for such Fiscal Year (and, if necessary, subsequent Fiscal Years) in an amount and manner sufficient to eliminate such deficit as quickly as possible.
>
> \(c\) *Section 704(c) Allocations.* In accordance with Section 704(c) of the Code and Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-3, items of income, gain, loss, and deduction with respect to any property contributed to the Company shall, solely for tax purposes, be allocated among the Members so as to take into account any variation between the adjusted tax basis of such property to the Company and its Fair Market Value at the time of contribution. The Company shall use the traditional method under Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-3(b) for purposes of such allocations.

**Section 6.03 --- Regulatory Allocations.** The following special allocations shall be made in the following order of priority: (a) minimum gain chargeback allocations in accordance with Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-2(f); and (b) partner nonrecourse debt minimum gain chargeback allocations in accordance with Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-2(i)(4). The provisions of this Section 6.03 are intended to comply with certain regulatory requirements set forth in Treasury Regulation Sections 1.704-1(b) and 1.704-2 and shall be interpreted consistently therewith.

**Section 6.04 --- Tax Allocations.** Items of Company taxable income, gain, loss, deduction, and credit shall be allocated among the Members for federal and state income tax purposes in a manner consistent with the allocations of the corresponding book items under this Article VI, except as otherwise required by Section 704(c) of the Code and the Treasury Regulations thereunder.

**Section 6.05 --- Characterization of Income.** Each item of income, gain, deduction, and loss allocated to the Members pursuant to this Article VI shall retain the same character (ordinary income, capital gain, Section 1231 gain or loss, or otherwise) as determined at the Company level. For the avoidance of doubt, the Company\'s assets --- including accounts receivable arising from data analytics and consulting services, proprietary data sets under development, and Intellectual Property --- generate items of income and gain that retain their character when allocated to and reported by the Members on their respective tax returns.

**[Article VII --- Distributions]{.underline}**

**Section 7.01 --- Distributions Generally.** Distributions shall be made at such times and in such amounts as determined by the Manager in its sole discretion, subject to the Company\'s then-current obligations, anticipated expenditures, and such reasonable reserves as the Manager deems necessary or appropriate.

**Section 7.02 --- Tax Distributions.** Within ninety (90) days following the end of each Fiscal Year, the Company shall distribute to each Member an amount equal to such Member\'s allocable share of the Company\'s taxable income for such Fiscal Year multiplied by the highest combined federal and state marginal income tax rate applicable to individuals residing in the State of North Carolina, to the extent the Company has available cash. Tax distributions made pursuant to this Section 7.02 shall be treated as advances against future distributions under Section 7.01.

**Section 7.03 --- Distribution Waterfall.** Subject to Section 7.02, distributions shall be made in the following order of priority:

> \(a\) first, to each Member in proportion to their respective Percentage Interests until each Member has received cumulative distributions equal to the aggregate amount of such Member\'s capital contributions to the Company; and
>
> \(b\) thereafter, to the Members in proportion to their respective Percentage Interests.

**Section 7.04 --- No Distribution in Kind.** No Member may demand or receive a distribution in kind except with the prior written consent of all Members.

**Section 7.05 --- Withholding.** The Company shall be entitled to withhold from distributions to any Member any amounts required to be withheld under applicable federal, state, or local tax law. Any amounts so withheld shall be treated as a distribution to such Member for all purposes of this Agreement.

**[Article VIII --- Transfers of Membership Interests]{.underline}**

**Section 8.01 --- General Restriction on Transfers.** No Member shall Transfer all or any portion of its Membership Interest except in compliance with this Article VIII. Any purported Transfer in violation of this Article VIII shall be null and void and of no force or effect, and the Company shall not recognize or give effect to any such purported Transfer on its books and records.

**Section 8.02 --- Permitted Transfers.** A Member may Transfer all or any portion of its Membership Interest without compliance with Section 8.03 to:

> \(a\) in the case of a Member who is an individual, to such Member\'s spouse, lineal descendants, or a trust for the benefit of such Member or such Member\'s spouse or lineal descendants; or
>
> \(b\) in the case of a Member that is an entity, to an Affiliate of such Member; provided, that such Affiliate remains an Affiliate of such Member at all times during which it holds such Membership Interest, and if such transferee ceases to be an Affiliate, such transferee shall immediately Transfer the Membership Interest back to the original transferring Member.

Any Permitted Transferee under this Section 8.02 shall, as a condition to such Transfer, execute a joinder agreement in form and substance satisfactory to the Manager, agreeing to be bound by all terms and conditions of this Agreement.

**Section 8.03 --- Right of First Refusal.**

> **(a) Triggering Events.** The right of first refusal (\"ROFR\") set forth in this Section 8.03 shall be triggered upon the occurrence of any of the following (each, a \"Triggering Event\"):
>
> \(i\) the proposed Transfer by any Member of all or any portion of its Membership Interest (other than a Permitted Transfer under Section 8.02); or
>
> \(ii\) a Change of Control of any Member that is an entity.
>
> **(b) ROFR Notice.** Upon the occurrence of a Triggering Event, the Transferring Member (or, in the case of a Change of Control, the Member that has undergone or proposes to undergo such Change of Control) shall deliver written notice (the \"ROFR Notice\") to the Company and each other Member (the \"Non-Transferring Members\") within ten (10) business days of the earlier of (x) the execution of a definitive agreement for such Transfer or Change of Control, or (y) the consummation of such Transfer or Change of Control. The ROFR Notice shall include:
>
> \(1\) a description of the proposed Transfer or Change of Control, including the structure and material terms of the transaction;
>
> \(2\) the identity of the proposed transferee or acquiror;
>
> \(3\) the proposed purchase price or, in the case of a Change of Control, the Fair Market Value of the Membership Interest as determined under Section 8.04; and
>
> \(4\) all other material terms and conditions of the proposed transaction.
>
> **(c) Exercise Period.** Each Non-Transferring Member shall have forty-five (45) calendar days following delivery of the ROFR Notice (the \"Exercise Period\") to elect to purchase all (but not less than all) of the Transferring Member\'s Membership Interest subject to the Triggering Event at the price set forth in the ROFR Notice (or, in the case of a Change of Control, at the Fair Market Value determined under Section 8.04), by delivering written notice of such election to the Transferring Member and the Company. If more than one Non-Transferring Member elects to exercise the ROFR, each exercising Non-Transferring Member shall purchase a pro rata share of such Membership Interest based on their respective Percentage Interests (determined without regard to the Transferring Member\'s interest).
>
> **(d) Closing of ROFR Purchase.** The closing of any purchase pursuant to this Section 8.03 shall occur within thirty (30) days following the expiration of the Exercise Period. Payment shall be made in cash at the closing. The Transferring Member shall deliver all documentation reasonably necessary to effect the Transfer, including executed assignment instruments and any required consents.
>
> **(e) Failure to Exercise; Waiver.** If no Non-Transferring Member elects to exercise the ROFR within the Exercise Period, the ROFR shall be deemed waived with respect to such Triggering Event, and the Transferring Member may consummate the proposed Transfer (or the Change of Control may be completed) on the terms described in the ROFR Notice. If the proposed Transfer or Change of Control is not consummated within ninety (90) days following the expiration of the Exercise Period, the ROFR shall again apply to any subsequent Transfer or Change of Control as though no ROFR Notice had been delivered.
>
> **(f) No Partial Exercise.** The ROFR may only be exercised with respect to all of the Membership Interest subject to the Triggering Event and may not be exercised as to less than all of such interest.

**Section 8.04 --- Fair Market Value Determination.**

> \(a\) The Fair Market Value of a Membership Interest for purposes of Section 8.03 shall be determined by mutual agreement of the Transferring Member and the electing Non-Transferring Member(s).
>
> \(b\) If the parties cannot agree on Fair Market Value within fifteen (15) days following the exercise of the ROFR, the Fair Market Value shall be determined by a nationally recognized independent valuation firm mutually acceptable to the parties. If the parties cannot agree on a valuation firm within ten (10) days following the expiration of such fifteen (15)-day period, each party shall select one nationally recognized independent valuation firm, and the two selected firms shall jointly select a third firm, which shall determine the Fair Market Value. The determination of such third firm shall be final and binding on the parties.
>
> \(c\) The costs of any independent valuation under this Section 8.04 shall be borne equally by the Transferring Member and the electing Non-Transferring Member(s).
>
> \(d\) The Fair Market Value shall reflect the value of the Membership Interest as a percentage of the total enterprise value of the Company, without discount for lack of control or lack of marketability, unless otherwise agreed by the parties in writing.

**[Article IX --- Management Services Agreement and IP License Agreement]{.underline}**

**Section 9.01 --- Management Services Agreement.** The Company and Prism Health Analytics, Inc. have entered into the Management Services Agreement dated August 1, 2018, pursuant to which Prism provides the Company with certain management, administrative, human resources, accounting, legal, information technology, and office-sharing services (the \"Management Services\"). The annual fee for the Management Services is Eight Hundred Fifty Thousand Dollars (\$850,000), payable in equal quarterly installments of Two Hundred Twelve Thousand Five Hundred Dollars (\$212,500) on the last business day of each calendar quarter. The fee shall be adjusted annually on January 1 of each year by the percentage increase (if any) in the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics for the prior calendar year, provided that the annual increase shall not exceed three percent (3%). The Management Services fee was established at the inception of this Agreement and has not been the subject of a benchmarking study, transfer pricing analysis, or independent appraisal at any time since execution. The fee does not adjust based on the actual volume, scope, or cost of services provided by Prism to the Company.

**Section 9.02 --- IP License Agreement.** The Company and Prism Health Analytics, Inc. have entered into the IP License Agreement dated August 1, 2018, pursuant to which Prism grants the Company a non-exclusive, non-transferable license to use certain Intellectual Property owned by Prism, including proprietary algorithms, software platforms, and data processing tools (the \"Licensed IP\"), in connection with the Company\'s Business. The annual license fee is Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$400,000), payable in equal quarterly installments of One Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$100,000) on the last business day of each calendar quarter. The license fee was established at the inception of this Agreement and, similar to the Management Services fee, has not been the subject of a benchmarking study, transfer pricing analysis, or independent appraisal at any time since execution. The license fee does not vary based on the Company\'s revenue, number of users, or volume of data processed through the Licensed IP. The IP License Agreement shall terminate automatically upon a Transfer of Prism\'s Membership Interest in the Company (including a deemed Transfer resulting from a Change of Control of Prism), unless the parties agree otherwise in writing.

**Section 9.03 --- Approval of Related-Party Transactions.** Any amendment or modification to the Management Services Agreement or the IP License Agreement, or any new transaction between the Company and Prism Health Analytics, Inc. or any Affiliate of Prism, shall require the prior written consent of Members holding at least a Majority Interest. For the avoidance of doubt, Prism Health Analytics, Inc., holding a Percentage Interest of 80.5%, constitutes a Majority Interest for purposes of this Section 9.03.

**[Article X --- \[Provisions Omitted\]]{.underline}**

\[Provisions regarding dissolution, winding up, and termination of the Company omitted.\]

**[Article XI --- Tax Matters]{.underline}**

**Section 11.01 --- Tax Classification.** The Company shall be treated as a partnership for federal and state income tax purposes. No Member shall take any action inconsistent with such classification without the prior written consent of all Members.

**Section 11.02 --- Partnership Representative.** Prism Health Analytics, Inc. shall serve as the \"Partnership Representative\" of the Company within the meaning of Section 6223 of the Code (as amended by the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2015). The Partnership Representative shall have the authority to act on behalf of the Company in connection with all federal, state, and local tax audits and proceedings, including the authority to bind the Company and the Members with respect to any tax matters.

**Section 11.03 --- Tax Returns.** The Company shall prepare and file (or cause to be prepared and filed) all required federal and state income tax returns, including IRS Form 1065 and all applicable state partnership returns. The Manager shall furnish to each Member a copy of Schedule K-1 (or the equivalent thereof) within ninety (90) days following the close of each Fiscal Year.

**Section 11.04 --- Tax Elections.** The Partnership Representative shall have the authority to make all tax elections on behalf of the Company, including:

> \(a\) an election under Section 754 of the Code to adjust the basis of Company property upon the transfer of a Membership Interest or upon a distribution to a Member; and
>
> \(b\) any election under Section 6226 of the Code (push-out election) in connection with any audit adjustment.

**Section 11.05 --- No Section 754 Election Currently in Effect.** As of the date of this Agreement, the Company has not made an election under Section 754 of the Code. Such an election shall only be made at the direction of the Partnership Representative in its sole discretion.

**[Article XII --- Miscellaneous Provisions]{.underline}**

**Section 12.01 --- Governing Law.** This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina, without giving effect to any choice or conflict of law provision or rule that would cause the application of the laws of any other jurisdiction.

**Section 12.02 --- Entire Agreement.** This Agreement, together with the schedules and exhibits attached hereto and the documents referenced herein, constitutes the entire agreement among the Members with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements, understandings, representations, and warranties, whether written or oral, relating to the Company.

**Section 12.03 --- Amendments.** This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by Members holding at least a Majority Interest.

**Section 12.04 --- Notices.** All notices, requests, demands, and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered to the Members at the addresses set forth on Schedule A, or to such other address as a Member may designate by written notice to the Company and the other Members.

**Section 12.05 --- Severability.** If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or unenforceable in any respect, such invalidity, illegality, or unenforceability shall not affect any other provision of this Agreement, and this Agreement shall be construed as if such invalid, illegal, or unenforceable provision had never been contained herein.

**Section 12.06 --- Counterparts.** This Agreement may be executed in any number of counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, and all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.

**Section 12.07 --- Waiver.** No waiver of any provision of this Agreement or of any right or remedy hereunder shall be effective unless in writing and signed by the waiving party. No waiver shall constitute a continuing waiver unless expressly stated in such written waiver.

result:
From: Derek Whitfield, Managing Director, Linden Rock Advisory,
 LLC <dwhitfield@lindenrockadvisory.com>
To: James Calloway, Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP <jcalloway@cswlaw.com>
Cc: Sandra Liu, Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP <sliu@cswlaw.com>; Tara Brennan,
 Linden Rock Advisory, LLC <tbrennan@lindenrockadvisory.com>
Date:
Subject: QSBS Eligibility — Angel Investor Shareholders / Prism Health
 Analytics Transaction
Content-Type: text/plain; charset="utf-8"
Content-Transfer-Encoding: quoted-printable
MIME-Version: 1.0

James,

I hope this note finds you well. I know everyone has been heads-down on dilig=
ence for the Prism Health Analytics, Inc. acquisition by Ridgeline Capital Pa=
rtners, and I appreciate the work your team has been putting in since we sign=
ed the LOI back on September 12. I'm writing today on behalf of several of Pr=
ism's angel investor shareholders who have raised an important tax question t=
hat we believe warrants formal analysis as part of the transaction structure =
work product.

As you know, the 14 individual angel investors and early employees who collec=
tively hold the remaining 20% of Prism common stock will be cashing out entir=
ely in this transaction. A group of these shareholders has asked Linden Rock =
to raise the question of whether their Prism stock qualifies as "qualified sm=
all business stock" under Section 1202 of the Internal Revenue Code =E2=80=94=
 and, critically, whether the QSBS exclusion will be available to them given =
the proposed transaction structure.

Let me lay out the relevant facts and questions as clearly as I can.

**Background:**

Several of the angel investors acquired their Prism common stock in 2016, sho=
rtly after the company was incorporated on June 14, 2016. At that time, Prism=
 was organized as a C corporation. Prism maintained C corporation status from=
 its date of incorporation through December 31, 2016 =E2=80=94 a period of ap=
proximately six and a half months =E2=80=94 before electing S corporation sta=
tus effective January 1, 2017. Prism has been an S corporation continuously s=
ince that date.

The investors understand that Section 1202 provides for up to 100% exclusion =
of gain on the sale of qualified small business stock acquired after Septembe=
r 27, 2010, provided the stock was held for more than five years, was issued =
by a C corporation at the time of issuance, and the issuing corporation met t=
he $50 million aggregate gross assets test at the time of stock issuance. The=
 investors have held their stock for over eight years at this point and belie=
ve the five-year holding period requirement is clearly satisfied, with closin=
g currently anticipated for early 2025.

The financial stakes here are significant. The total consideration allocable =
to the 14 other shareholders is $74,000,000, all cash. Individual shareholder=
s in this group could face gains in the millions of dollars. The QSBS exclusi=
on =E2=80=94 if available =E2=80=94 would be extremely material to their afte=
r-tax proceeds from this transaction.

**Questions for Analysis:**

The shareholders and our team have identified the following specific question=
s that we would like your firm to address:

1. **C-to-S Conversion:** Does the fact that Prism converted from C corporati=
on to S corporation status after only approximately six and a half months (th=
e entirety of 2016) affect QSBS eligibility? Is stock that was issued by a co=
rporation while it was a C corporation, but which subsequently elected S corp=
oration status, still considered "qualified small business stock" within the =
meaning of Section 1202?

2. **Interaction with Section 338(h)(10) Election:** This is the question tha=
t concerns the investors most. The proposed transaction structure contemplate=
s a Section 338(h)(10) election, which as we understand it treats the transac=
tion as a deemed asset sale rather than a stock sale for federal income tax p=
urposes. Section 1202 by its terms applies to gain on the "sale or exchange" =
of "qualified small business stock." If the 338(h)(10) election recharacteriz=
es the transaction as a deemed asset sale, does the QSBS exclusion still appl=
y to the shareholders' gains? Or does the deemed asset sale treatment effecti=
vely eliminate the shareholders' ability to claim the exclusion?

3. **Qualified Small Business Requirements:** Did Prism meet the "qualified s=
mall business" requirements at the time of stock issuance =E2=80=94 specifica=
lly, the $50 million aggregate gross assets test under Section 1202(d)? We be=
lieve the answer is clearly yes given Prism's size in 2016, but we want to co=
nfirm that the analysis has been done.

4. **California Residents:** Two of the 14 shareholders are California reside=
nts. We are aware that California does not conform to the federal Section 120=
2 exclusion, but the investors would like to understand their federal tax pos=
ition clearly, separate and apart from the state-level issue.

James, given the materiality of this issue to the selling shareholders, we ar=
e requesting that Calloway, Stern & Whitaker provide a written analysis of QS=
BS eligibility =E2=80=94 ideally as a dedicated section in the transaction st=
ructure memorandum your team is preparing, or alternatively as a standalone m=
emo. Several of the angel investors are making investment and personal financ=
ial planning decisions based on their understanding of the tax treatment, and=
 they need a definitive answer before they sign the definitive purchase agree=
ment.

I recognize this adds to the workload, and I appreciate your team's willingne=
ss to take it on. This is time-sensitive =E2=80=94 we would like to have the =
analysis in hand before the shareholders are asked to commit to the definitiv=
e documents. If you could aim to have something to us by early December, that=
 would be very helpful.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you need any additional information fro=
m us or from the Prism management team to complete the analysis. Tara Brennan=
 on our side can coordinate any diligence requests related to this.

Many thanks, James. I look forward to your response.

Best regards,

Derek Whitfield
Managing Director
Linden Rock Advisory, LLC
Direct: (704) 555-3180
dwhitfield@lindenrockadvisory.com

From: James Calloway <jcalloway@cswlaw.com>

om>
Date: November 20, 2024
Subject: RE: QSBS Eligibility =E2=80=94 Angel Investor Shareholders / Prism H=
ealth Analytics Transaction

Derek,

Thank you for raising this. The QSBS question is an important one, and I appr=
eciate you laying out the facts and issues as clearly as you have. It will ma=
ke our analysis considerably more efficient.

You've identified the right pressure points. The QSBS analysis here involves =
several layers of complexity, and I want to be candid that this is not an are=
a where we can give you a quick-and-easy answer. The key complicating factors=
, as I see them, are:

First, Prism's period as a C corporation was quite brief =E2=80=94 only appro=
ximately six and a half months, from incorporation on June 14, 2016 through D=
ecember 31, 2016. The stock was issued during that window, which is favorable=
 for Section 1202 purposes since QSBS must be issued by a C corporation. But =
the subsequent S corporation election effective January 1, 2017 =E2=80=94 and=
 Prism's continuous S corp status since that date =E2=80=94 raises the questi=
on of whether the stock retains its character as "qualified small business st=
ock" through the date of sale. There is authority suggesting that the QSBS ch=
aracter attaches at the time of original issuance and is not lost by a subseq=
uent conversion, but this warrants careful analysis given the facts here.

Second, and this is the issue I expect will require the most attention: the i=
nteraction between Section 1202 and the proposed Section 338(h)(10) election.=
 As you correctly note, the 338(h)(10) election recharacterizes what is econo=
mically a stock sale as a deemed asset sale for federal income tax purposes. =
Section 1202, by its terms, provides the exclusion for gain recognized on the=
 "sale or exchange" of "qualified small business stock." There is a real ques=
tion =E2=80=94 and I want to flag this as a preliminary observation, not a co=
nclusion =E2=80=94 as to whether the deemed asset sale treatment under Sectio=
n 338(h)(10) is in tension with the statutory language of Section 1202. If th=
e shareholders are treated for tax purposes as having sold assets (through th=
e deemed liquidation of the S corporation) rather than having sold stock, it =
is not immediately clear that the gain they recognize qualifies for the Secti=
on 1202 exclusion. The direct guidance on this specific interaction is limite=
d, and we will need to work through the statutory mechanics carefully.

I have asked Sandra Liu to begin researching the issue. We intend to include =
a QSBS analysis as part of the transaction structure memorandum we are prepar=
ing for Ridgeline. I recognize that our primary engagement is with Ridgeline =
as the buyer, and the selling shareholders are not our clients, but the QSBS =
question is directly relevant to the overall transaction structure analysis a=
nd the allocation of tax consequences among the parties. It is entirely appro=
priate =E2=80=94 and, frankly, necessary =E2=80=94 for the memorandum to addr=
ess how the proposed structure affects the selling shareholders' ability to c=
laim the exclusion, particularly given the dollar amounts at stake.

We will work to have a substantive analysis to you in the coming weeks. My go=
al is to have the QSBS section drafted and incorporated into the memorandum w=
ell before the purchase agreement is executed, so that the shareholders have =
the information they need to make an informed decision.

I'll circle back as the analysis develops. In the meantime, if you have any a=
dditional detail on the specific dates of stock issuance to the individual an=
gel investors (we have some of this from diligence, but a clean schedule woul=
d be helpful), please have Tara send it over to Sandra and we will make sure =
it is reflected in the analysis.

Thank you again for raising this, Derek. More to come.

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

{"file_path": "report.md", "content": "# Tax Issue Identification Report

## Review of the Transaction Structure Memorandum — Acquisition of Prism Health Analytics, Inc.

**Prepared by:** Tax Review Function

**Date of Review:** February 2025

**Subject Document Reviewed:** "Tax Structure Memorandum — Acquisition of Prism Health Analytics, Inc.," prepared by Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP, dated February 10, 2025 (the "Memo").

**Supporting Documents Cross-Referenced:**

1. Agreement and Plan of Merger, dated January 28, 2025 (Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP / Ridgeline Acquisition Sub, Inc. / Prism Health Analytics, Inc. / Shareholders) — selected excerpts (the "PA").
2. Tax Due Diligence Report, Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP, dated January 15, 2025 (the "Thornfield Report").
3. Preliminary Purchase Price Allocation / Asset Valuation Summary, Thornfield Accounting Group, LLP (the "Asset Valuation Summary").
4. Prism Health Analytics, Inc. Capitalization Table, as of December 31, 2024 (the "Cap Table").
5. Amended and Restated Operating Agreement of Beacon Insights, LLC, effective August 1, 2018 (the "Beacon OA").
6. Email thread re: QSBS Eligibility — Angel Investor Shareholders, Linden Rock Advisory, LLC and Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP, November 2024 – January 2025 (the "QSBS Email Thread").

**Privilege Note:** This report is prepared in connection with the referenced transaction and is intended for the use of the addressee and its advisors. It identifies issues, inconsistencies, and omissions in the Memo when measured against the supporting deal documents; it does not itself constitute a tax opinion.

---

## I. Executive Summary

This report reviews the Tax Structure Memorandum prepared by Calloway, Stern & Whitaker LLP ("CSW") for Ridgeline Capital Partners Fund III, LP against the six supporting deal documents listed above. The review identified **thirty-three (33) distinct issues**, ranked by severity as follows: **3 Critical, 8 High, 10 Moderate, and 12 Low**.

The three Critical issues are, in our judgment, matters that must be resolved before the transaction closes and before any tax return (including Form 8023 and Form 8883) is filed:

1. **The Aggregate Deemed Sale Price (ADSP) is overstated by $48 million.** The Memo computes ADSP as Enterprise Value ($425M) plus funded debt ($48M) = $473M. This double-counts the $48M of funded debt, because enterprise value already embeds debt (EV = equity value + net debt). Under Treas. Reg. 1.338-4, ADSP begins from the amount realized on the stock ($370M equity value) plus target liabilities. The Asset Valuation Summary computes ADSP at $425M. The $48M overstatement flows directly into the Class VII goodwill residual, inflating goodwill from approximately $196.3M (per Thornfield) to $272.8M (per the Memo) — a $76.5M goodwill variance when combined with the Class V understatement described below.

2. **The Memo asserts that there are no ineligible S corporation shareholders, directly contradicting the diligence record.** The Memo states that "all shareholders of Prism are U.S. individuals, and we are not aware of any ineligible shareholders." The Thornfield Report flags S corporation eligibility as a **Critical** open item for restricted stock holders, and the Cap Table marks three restricted stock holders as "Unconfirmed." Valid S corporation status is a threshold requirement for the Section 338(h)(10) election; if the S election was inadvertently terminated, the entire transaction structure collapses and cascading C corporation tax consequences arise. The Memo does not flag this contingency.

3. **The QSBS / Section 1202 analysis is entirely absent from the Memo.** The QSBS Email Thread documents an explicit request by the selling shareholders' advisor for a written QSBS analysis to be included in the Memo *before* the purchase agreement was signed, and CSW agreed to include it. The purchase agreement was signed January 28, 2025; the Memo is dated February 10, 2025; and the Memo contains no QSBS analysis whatsoever. The issue is material — several angel investors acquired shares during the 2016 C corporation period and may be eligible for a 100% Section 1202 exclusion, a seven-figure swing in after-tax proceeds — and the Section 338(h)(10) deemed asset sale framework may foreclose that exclusion, which is itself a critical input to the election decision.

Two additional themes run through the High-severity findings. **First, the Memo's purchase price allocation diverges materially from Thornfield's asset-level valuation** (developed technology stated at $19M versus $41.8M; government software licenses omitted entirely; aggregate tax basis stated at $42.8M versus $64.3M implied by the asset detail). Taken together with the ADSP error, the Memo's deemed sale gain of $430.2M is overstated by approximately **$69.5M** relative to a Thornfield-consistent computation ($360.7M), materially overstating the tax the Memo tells shareholders to expect. **Second, the Memo is in direct tension with the operative transaction documents** on several points: its escrow installment-sale recommendation contradicts PA Section 7.4 (which reports the escrow as amount realized in the year of closing); it glosses over the Beacon operating agreement's change-of-control ROFR (a PA closing condition) and the automatic termination of the Beacon IP license upon Prism's change of control; and its post-closing "check-the-box" recommendation is legally flawed because a Delaware corporation is a per se corporation that cannot elect partnership/disregarded classification.

The full severity-ranked catalog follows. Each issue identifies the source, the problem, why it matters, and a recommended remediation.

---

## II. Scope and Methodology

We reviewed the Memo section by section and cross-referenced each factual assertion, figure, defined term, and legal conclusion against the six supporting documents. We treated the PA as the operative transaction document, the Thornfield Report and Asset Valuation Summary as the diligence record, the Cap Table as the equity record of truth, the Beacon OA as the governing document for the Beacon subsidiary, and the QSBS Email Thread as the record of the selling shareholders' express requests and CSW's commitments. Issues were ranked by the magnitude of their potential tax, structural, or diligence consequences and by the degree to which they contradict the controlling documents. We did not independently verify facts outside the document set; where a figure could not be reconciled to any source, we identify it as a data gap rather than asserting a corrected value.

---

## III. Critical Issues

### C-1. ADSP Overstated by $48 Million — Double-Counting of Funded Debt

**Severity:** Critical

**Sources:** Memo §I, §IV.B, §V.A, Appendix B; Asset Valuation Summary, "Allocation Summary" tab (Note 1); PA §1.1 (defined terms: Enterprise Value, Equity Value, Funded Indebtedness).

**Issue:** The Memo computes the Aggregate Deemed Sale Price as:

> Enterprise Value ($425,000,000) + Assumed Liabilities / Funded Debt ($48,000,000) = **ADSP $473,000,000**.

This is incorrect. Enterprise value already includes the funded debt (EV = equity value + net debt; here $425M = $370M equity + $48M debt + $7M seller expenses). Adding the $48M of funded debt a second time double-counts it. Under Treas. Reg. 1.338-4, ADSP is the grossed-up amount realized on the sale of the target's **stock** plus the liabilities of old target. The amount realized on the stock is the equity value of $370,000,000 ($333M cash + $37M rollover equity), not the enterprise value. The Asset Valuation Summary computes ADSP at $425,000,000 ($370M + $48M + $7M seller expenses, treated conservatively as target liabilities).

The Memo also misapplies its own stated formula. Section IV.B says ADSP equals "(i) the grossed-up amount realized on the sale of the target's stock and (ii) the liabilities of old target," but then substitutes enterprise value for "amount realized on the stock" — an internal inconsistency.

**Why it matters:** The $48M overstatement inflates the entire purchase price allocation. It flows directly into the Class VII goodwill residual, which the Memo puts at $272.8M versus Thornfield's $196.3M. Goodwill amortization (Section 197, 15 years) is correspondingly overstated by approximately $5.1M per year ($18.19M vs. $13.09M), overstating Buyer's projected tax shield. The overstated ADSP also inflates the deemed sale gain reported to selling shareholders (see C-1 together with H-2). Because the parties must file Form 8883 consistent with the final allocation (PA §7.3(b)), an unreconciled ADSP creates a filing-position dispute and potential inconsistency between Buyer's and Sellers' returns.

**Recommendation:** Recompute ADSP from the amount realized on the stock ($370M) plus target liabilities, reconcile to the Asset Valuation Summary, and revise the Class VII residual and all downstream D&A and gain computations before Form 8883 is prepared. Confirm whether the $7M of seller transaction expenses are properly treated as target liabilities (see L-6).

### C-2. S Corporation Shareholder Eligibility Unconfirmed — Memo Contradicts the Diligence Record

**Severity:** Critical

**Sources:** Memo §II.A (Restricted Stock), §III.A ("we are not aware of any ineligible shareholders"); Thornfield Report §III.B (Critical Finding), §XI (Finding 1); Cap Table (S Corp Eligible Shareholder? column — three "Unconfirmed"); PA §4.9(a) (S corporation rep), §2.5 (restricted stock rep), §8.2(c) (closing condition).

**Issue:** The Memo states that "[n]o events causing a termination of the S election have been identified. All shareholders of Prism are U.S. individuals, and we are not aware of any ineligible shareholders." This directly contradicts the diligence record:

- The Thornfield Report identifies S corporation eligibility as a **Critical** open item for restricted stock holders, specifically (a) a Canadian national on an H-1B visa whose substantial-presence-test compliance has not been verified for all years, and (b) a possible transfer of shares to a family LLC that would be an ineligible shareholder.
- The Cap Table marks **three** restricted stock holders as "Unconfirmed": Lin Wei Zhang (H-1B visa holder, PRC citizen), Yusuf Al-Rashidi (dual U.S./Jordan citizen, documentation not on file), and Nina Petrova (Bulgarian-born, green-card status unconfirmed).
- The PA nonetheless represents (§4.9(a)) that "each shareholder is an eligible S corporation shareholder" and (§2.5) that each restricted stock holder "is an individual who is a citizen or resident of the United States," and makes maintenance of valid S status a closing condition (§8.2(c)).

There is also an internal inconsistency *between* the diligence documents: the Thornfield Report identifies two eligibility concerns (the H-1B holder and the family-LLC transfer), while the Cap Table identifies three different/unverified holders (Zhang, Al-Rashidi, Petrova) and does not clearly map to the "family LLC" concern. The Memo resolves none of this and instead asserts the all-clear.

**Why it matters:** Valid S corporation status is a threshold requirement for the Section 338(h)(10) election. If the S election was inadvertently terminated in any prior year (e.g., by a nonresident alien holder or an ineligible LLC holder), Prism would have been a C corporation for that and all subsequent years, the 338(h)(10) election would be unavailable, and cascading entity-level federal and state C corporation tax (plus penalties and interest) would arise for all open years. The Memo's "no BIG tax" conclusion (§III.B), its pass-through gain analysis (§V), and its state-tax analysis (§VI) all silently depend on continuous valid S status. The Memo's blanket eligibility assertion is both unsupported by and contrary to the record, and it understates the single largest structural risk in the transaction.

**Recommendation:** Before closing, obtain definitive documentation of every shareholder's eligibility for every year since January 1, 2017 — including immigration/residency records for the H-1B holder, citizenship documentation for Al-Rashidi, green-card confirmation for Petrova, and the operating agreement and member composition of any family LLC. Reconcile the Thornfield Report and Cap Table eligibility findings. Revise the Memo to flag S-election validity as a contingent assumption and to describe the consequences of an inadvertent termination. Consider whether the PA's S corporation representations (§4.9(a), §2.5) are adequately supported or require qualification, and whether specific indemnity/escrow coverage is warranted.

### C-3. QSBS / Section 1202 Analysis Entirely Omitted

**Severity:** Critical

**Sources:** Memo (no QSBS / Section 1202 content anywhere); QSBS Email Thread (Whitfield request; Calloway commitment of Nov. 20, 2024; Whitfield follow-up of Jan. 6, 2025); Thornfield Report §VIII.B (Moderate Finding 4); Cap Table (per-shareholder "Potential QSBS — not analyzed" notes); PA §7.3(a) (338(h)(10) election).

**Issue:** The Memo contains no analysis of Section 1202 qualified small business stock (QSBS). This is a complete omission of an analysis that was (a) expressly requested by the selling shareholders' financial advisor, (b) expressly committed to by CSW, and (c) promised *before* the purchase agreement was signed:

- The QSBS Email Thread shows Linden Rock Advisory requesting, on behalf of several angel investors, a written QSBS analysis "ideally as a dedicated section in the transaction structure memorandum," before the shareholders committed to the definitive documents.
- CSW responded on November 20, 2024 that it would "include a QSBS analysis as part of the transaction structure memorandum" and that its "goal is to have the QSBS section drafted and incorporated into the memorandum well before the purchase agreement is executed."
- On January 6, 2025, Linden Rock followed up to state that no analysis had been received and to reiterate the request, adding a specific question about the escrow component.
- The PA was signed January 28, 2025; the Memo is dated February 10, 2025. The promised analysis was never delivered, and the shareholders signed the definitive agreement without it.

The Cap Table confirms that the two founders and four seed-round angel investors acquired shares during the 2016 C corporation period (June 14 – December 31, 2016) and are potentially QSBS-eligible, while shares issued after January 1, 2017 are not (Section 1202(c)(1) requires issuance by a C corporation). The Thornfield Report (§VIII.B) flags the interaction of Section 1202 with the 338(h)(10) deemed asset sale as unresolved and recommends a definitive analysis by tax counsel.

**Why it matters:** Section 1202 can exclude up to 100% of gain on qualified small business stock held more than five years. For the affected shareholders, the difference between a 100% federal exclusion and full capital-gains taxation is, in several cases, a seven-figure swing in after-tax proceeds. Critically, the Section 338(h)(10) election recharacterizes the transaction as a deemed asset sale followed by a deemed liquidation rather than a stock sale; because Section 1202 applies to gain on the "sale or exchange" of QSBS, the deemed asset sale treatment may foreclose the exclusion. This means the QSBS question is not merely a shareholder-side concern — it is a direct input to the election decision itself. If the 338(h)(10) election eliminates QSBS for eligible holders, an alternative structure (e.g., a stock sale without the election) may be value-maximizing for those shareholders, and the Memo's failure to analyze this deprives the parties of information needed to evaluate the trade-off. The Memo's silence is also a failure to deliver on an express, documented commitment.

**Recommendation:** Prepare and circulate a definitive QSBS analysis immediately, addressing: (i) whether shares issued during the 2016 C corporation period retain QSBS character through the intervening S corporation years and the sale; (ii) whether the 338(h)(10) deemed asset sale / deemed liquidation forecloses the Section 1202 exclusion; (iii) the $50M aggregate-gross-assets test at issuance; (iv) the per-taxpayer/per-issuer limitations; (v) the California non-conformance issue for the two California-resident holders; and (vi) the escrow component (whether any exclusion applies to gain attributable to escrow amounts released after closing). The analysis should be delivered to the selling shareholders and should inform any reconsideration of the election or the structure.

### H-2. Aggregate Adjusted Tax Basis Inconsistent with the Asset Detail ($42.8M vs. $64.3M)

**Issue:** The Memo computes the total deemed sale gain as ADSP ($473M) less "Aggregate Adjusted Tax Basis of Assets" ($42.8M) = $430.2M, and states in §III.B that Prism's "current aggregate adjusted tax basis in its assets … is approximately $42,800,000." ### H-5. Beacon Interest — Section 751 "Hot Assets," Section 754 Election, and Look-Through Analysis Omitted

**Severity:** High

**Sources:** Memo §V.A ("gain on the deemed disposition of Prism's Beacon Insights interest is expected to be long-term capital gain"), §VIII; Thornfield Report §VI.B (hot-asset observation); Asset Valuation Summary, Asset A-009 and Note 2 (no look-through; 754 election decision); Beacon OA §11.04, §11.05 (754 election authority; no 754 currently in effect).

**Issue:** The Memo treats the entire $22.1M gain on the deemed disposition of Prism's 80.5% Beacon interest as "expected to be long-term capital gain." It does not address three items flagged in the diligence record:

- **Section 751 "hot assets."** The Thornfield Report (§VI.B) notes that Beacon's underlying assets include accounts receivable (approximately $2.1M) and accrued but unbilled revenue (approximately $1.8M), which should be analyzed under Section 751 for hot-asset characterization in connection with the deemed transfer of Prism's partnership interest. Under Section 751, the portion of the gain attributable to "unrealized receivables" (including AR and unbilled revenue) is recharacterized as ordinary income, not capital gain. The Memo's blanket capital-gain assertion may therefore be incorrect.
- **Section 754 election.** The Asset Valuation Summary (Note 2) states that the amortization/depreciation treatment of the step-up allocated to the Beacon interest "will depend on the nature of Beacon's underlying assets and whether a §754 election is made by Beacon," and recommends that Buyer's counsel determine whether a 754 election is advisable and prepare the Section 743(b) basis-adjustment computations. The Beacon OA (§11.04, §11.05) confirms the Partnership Representative has 754 authority and that no 754 election is currently in effect. The Memo does not address the 754 decision.
- **Look-through analysis.** Thornfield has not performed a look-through of Beacon's underlying assets; the Memo does not flag this gap or its consequences for the character and amortization of the step-up.

**Why it matters:** The 751 hot-asset recharacterization could convert a portion of the $22.1M gain from capital gain (23.8%) to ordinary income (up to 37% plus NIIT), a material tax difference that flows through to all Prism shareholders. The 754 election directly affects Buyer's inside basis in Beacon's assets and the usability of the step-up. The Memo's "long-term capital gain" conclusion is presented without the analysis needed to support it.

**Recommendation:** Perform the Section 751 hot-asset analysis on Beacon's AR and unbilled revenue; quantify any ordinary-income recharacterization and revise the gain character accordingly. Analyze whether a Beacon 754 election is advisable and prepare the 743(b) computations. Complete (or commission) the Beacon look-through and reflect it in the PPA and the buyer D&A schedule.

**Sources:** Memo §VIII (cursory ROFR mention; no IP license termination discussion); Beacon OA §8.03(a)(ii) (ROFR triggered by Change of Control of a member), §8.03(b)–(f) (ROFR mechanics), §9.02 (IP license auto-terminates on Prism change of control); PA §8.2(g) (consents under Beacon OA as closing condition).

If exercised, Prism's Beacon interest could be purchased by the minority members, frustrating the Buyer's acquisition of an asset allocated $28.5M in the PPA. - **Automatic IP license termination.** Beacon OA §9.02 provides that the IP License Agreement "shall terminate automatically upon a Transfer of Prism's Membership Interest in the Company (including a deemed Transfer resulting from a Change of Control of Prism), unless the parties agree otherwise in writing." Beacon relies on Prism's licensed IP to generate its approximately $11M of annual revenue. The Memo does not mention this automatic termination at all. If the IP license terminates at closing, Beacon's revenue-generating capability and the value of the $28.5M allocated interest are impaired, and Beacon's minority members are affected.

Analyze whether the 338(h)(10) deemed transfer itself triggers the Beacon OA transfer provisions. Revise the Memo to treat these as tax/structural items, not background notes.

### H-7. Intercompany Transfer Pricing — $6.3M Section 482 Exposure Not Addressed; PA Representation Potentially Inaccurate

### H-8. Post-Closing "Check-the-Box" Recommendation Legally Flawed — A Delaware Corporation Cannot Check-the-Box

**Severity:** High

**Sources:** Memo §VII.B ("converting Prism from a C corporation to a disregarded entity or partnership for federal income tax purposes following the closing, by making a check-the-box election under Treas. Reg. 1.301-7701-3"), §IX.B.4, §IX.C.4.

**Issue:** The Memo recommends that Buyer convert post-closing Prism (the Surviving Corporation, a Delaware corporation) into a disregarded entity or partnership "by making a check-the-box election." This is not available. Under the entity-classification regulations, a business entity incorporated under a state corporate statute is a "per se corporation" (Treas. Reg. 301.7701-2(b)) and is not an "eligible entity" that may elect its classification under Treas. Reg. 301.7701-3. A Delaware corporation therefore cannot use a check-the-box election to become a disregarded entity or partnership. Achieving flow-through treatment would require an actual liquidation of the corporation (e.g., a taxable liquidation under Sections 331/336, with corporate-level tax on the appreciated stepped-up assets) or a non-taxable liquidation under Sections 332/337 (available only into an 80%-or-greater corporate parent — inapplicable here because Fund III is a partnership), followed by contribution of the assets to a new LLC/partnership. The Memo's recommendation, as written, rests on a legal error and understates the cost and complexity of achieving flow-through treatment.

**Why it matters:** The recommendation is presented to the Investment Committee as a straightforward post-closing efficiency step. If relied upon, it will not work as described, and the actual path (a taxable corporate liquidation) could trigger corporate-level tax on the very stepped-up basis assets the 338(h)(10) election was designed to create — potentially undermining a portion of the election's benefit. The Memo also cites the regulation as "Treas. Reg. 1.301-7701-3," a malformed citation (see L-3).

**Recommendation:** Withdraw or correct the check-the-box recommendation. If flow-through treatment is desired, analyze the actual mechanics (taxable liquidation vs. alternative structures), quantify the corporate-level tax cost, and present the trade-off. Correct the regulatory citation.
